segments.title: Royal Navy interwar mutinies, pay, and command
segments.summary: The Patreon live lecture connects Royal Navy mutinies to pay disputes, food standards, divisional channels, chief petty officer networks, and officers' handling of formal orders.
segments.body: The discussion captures the episode's organizing argument. Dr. Clarke frames interwar Royal Navy mutinies less as revolutionary uprisings than as institutional crises shaped by pay, provisions, war weariness, communications, and careful command practice. The discussion moves from why wartime grievances often stopped short of formal mutiny, through 1919 cases such as HMS Kilbride and the First Destroyer Flotilla, to Invergordon as a fleet-wide pay crisis where officers and sailors often understood the dispute in similar terms.

evidence.note: The opening defines the lecture around interwar Royal Navy mutinies and explains how pay, food, divisional reporting, chief networks, and the technical definition of refusing orders shaped mutiny cases.
The 1919 section contrasts scattered postwar refusals such as HMS Kilbride with Invergordon's much larger economic and political effect.
The First Destroyer Flotilla section links Baltic service orders, volunteer-language confusion, and chief-organized signaling to sailors leaving their posts.
The Invergordon transition introduces the later question of why similar 20th-century mutinies did not recur and identifies the 10 percent and 25 percent pay-cut problem.
The resolution section describes Admiralty engagement at Invergordon and the ships returning to normal routine and leaving as ordered.
The HMS Nelson report shows officers avoiding a direct order to weigh anchor and treating the dispute as a pay claim against government rather than a mutiny against officers.
